title: ugput
html:
ugput

  • Last UpdatedDez 30, 2022
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

Initialize an occurrence of the specified entity for subsequent calls to ugputc. 

short ugput (unsigned char*entity, unsigned char*buffer, shortlen);

Parameters

entity
The name of the entity you want to work on. A value of NULL, or an empty string, denotes the current entity. The entity must be painted on the component and it must be defined in the application model.
buffer
The data to be used. This data must be in DBMS storage format.
len
The size of the data pointed to by buffer. This value must be between zero (inclusive) and 8192 (exclusive), and must not exceed the size of the buffer.

Return Values

Table 1. Values returned by ugput
Value Meaning
4 The occurrence was found in the component. The current occurrence is removed and the cursor is repositioned to the found occurrence.
The occurrence was found among the removed occurrences; it was unremoved.
2 Entity is painted as an up entity and one hit was found in the database.
1 Entity is painted as an up entity with code in the writeUp trigger and the key value was not found during the database lookup. It is assumed that this is a new occurrence.
0 A new occurrence was created.
-1 Depending on the situation:
  • Entity is not painted on the component.
  • Entity is a dummy entity.
  • Unexpected end of file encountered.
-2 Entity is painted as an up entity with no code in the writeUp trigger and the key value was not found during the database lookup.
-3 Exceptional I/O error.
-4 Open request for the table or file failed.
-7 The key exists in the database but was not found in the hitlist. This occurs when the user tries to enter a duplicate key.
-11 The occurrence is currently locked.
-14 Entity is painted as a normal ‘down’ entity and multiple hits were found during the database lookup (ambiguous key).
-15 Entity is painted as an up entity and multiple hits were found during the database lookup.
-16 DBMS or network error.

Use

Library: yrtl

Description

ugput initializes an occurrence of the specified entity, so that it can be filled by one of more calls to ugputc. These two functions should always be used together. If entity is NULL or is an empty string, the current entity is used.

The data pointed to by buffer must be in DBMS-specific format. This means that the layout of the buffer can be database-dependent. Some database drivers use different alignment/padding than others, and you must have the exact layout of the data buffer. The best way to find this out is to do a retrieve on your entity, using /pri=255 (or $ioprint=255 in the assignment file) and to look at the open description block and the I/O buffer dump (see the example below).

Functionally, calling ugput is equivalent to creating a new occurrence, entering the primary key value(s), and leaving the primary key field(s), so that a primary key lookup (retrieve/o) is done. Depending on the result of ugput, you can then call ugputc to fill the new occurrence with data. Note that ugput itself does not fill the occurrence, even if you provide all the data in the buffer; it just does the primary key logic.

When ugput is called, Uniface first creates an empty occurrence of the specified or current entity in the external structure and makes it current. What happens next depends on the value of the len parameter:

  • If len is zero, and entity is a painted model entity, ugput returns a value of zero without doing a primary key lookup.
  • If len is greater than zero, but the buffer does not contain all of the primary key data, ugput returns -1.
  • If len is greater than zero, and the buffer contains all the primary key data, ugput performs a retrieve/o (primary key lookup) to check the existence of this occurrence, and returns the status of retrieve/o. Note that the values that can be returned by ugput are the same as those of retrieve/o: a non-negative status means success, a negative status means an error.
    • If the retrieve/o succeeds, ugput clears the occurrence (including the primary key data), and returns the non-negative value. You must then call ugputc to provide the data for the new occurrence.
    • If the retrieve/o fails, ugputc returns the negative error status. In this case, it will not be possible to call ugputc successfully.

In keeping with the retrieve/o behavior, ugput acts differently when data has been retrieved and a hitlist is present, than it does when data has not been retrieved and no hitlist is present. The following table summarizes the action performed, and status returned (in parentheses), in various situations:

Table 2. Behavior of ugput
New occurrence Number of matching occurrences in the DBMS:
is ... None One Many
Not in hitlist Create new occurrence (0) Error (-7) Error (-14)
One hit in hitlist Make current (3 or 4) Make current (3 or 4) Make current (4)
Many hits in hitlist Make first current (3 or 4) Make first current (3 or 4) Make first current (4)
In component, but no hitlist present Make current (3 or 4) Retrieve (3 or 4) Retrieve first (4)
Not in component, but no hitlist present Create new occurrence (0) Retrieve (4) Retrieve first (4)

There are a number of things to keep in mind when you work with ugput and ugputc:

  • The layout of the buffer to be used might be dependent on the database driver to which your entity is mapped. It might also be dependent on specific assignments for this driver (USYS$XXX_PARAMS). Use /pri=255 and check your message frame to see the exact layout.
  • Creating a new occurrence with ugput and ugputc does not set the occurrence modification status for that occurrence. So if you directly follow this by a store, Uniface does not know that the database has been modified, and will not store the new occurrence. You must modify the new occurrence for Uniface to store it.
  • You cannot call ugputc without first having called ugput. However, you can call ugput with a zero-length buffer to suppress the primary key lookup.
  • When you call ugput with a certain primary key value, nothing stops you from offering a different primary key to the following ugputc. Uniface does not check this, and what you will see in the component is the primary key value that was passed to ugputc. This is not recommended programming practice.
Note: A more modern approach to fill an occurrence from 3GL would be to put the code in a 4GL operation, pass the field values as parameters, and activate that operation from 3GL. Doing this will avoid all the pitfalls mentioned above.

Example

The following example illustrates the use of ugput and ugputc in creating a new occurrence of an entity ENT with the following fields:

Table 3. Example fields for ugput and ugputc
Field name Data type Packing code
PK1 String C3
PK2 String C3
FINT Numeric I4
FLOAT Floating point F8
DESC String C20

PK1 and PK2 together form the primary key. The entity has been mapped neatly to a C structure, which is possible because this entity uses only very basic packing codes. The #pragma pack(1) is specific for the Microsoft Windows C compiler, so the fields will not be aligned in the structure (without this pragma, the size of this struct would be 48 instead of the expected 38).

This example was tested with the Uniface Sequential (SEQ) driver. With another database, the required record layout might be different, for example, containing filler bytes between the fields. Statements for error handling have been omitted for the sake of brevity.

#include <h3gl.h>
#pragma pack(1) /* Prevent alignment in record buffer */

EXPORT void INSERT_OCC ()
{
struct
{
char pk1[3];
char pk2[3];
int fint;
double ffloat;
char fc20[20];
}
ent;

memcpy(&ent.pk1, "xyz789", 6);

/* Provide only primary key data here */
ret = ugput("", (unsigned char*)&ent, 6);
if ( ret < 0 )
return;

uputmess(ret==0 ? "Inserting new occ." : "Updating existing occ.");

ent.fint = 123456789;
ent.ffloat = 3.1415926535;
memcpy(ent.fc20, "01234567890123456789", 20);

/* Supply complete occurrence data */
ugputc("", (unsigned char*)&ent, sizeof(ent));
}
Related Links
  • ugget

    Move data from the current occurrence of the specified entity or current entity into a program buffer.

  • ugputc

    Copy data into an occurrence of the specified entity, which has been initialized by ugput.

  • retrieve/o

    Attempt to locate an occurrence of an entity using the current primary key value.
